content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 22, 2017

Nancy Diamond
City Attorney
822 G Street, Suite 3
Arcata, CA 95521

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-134

Dear Ms. Diamond:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Arcata City Councilmember Paul
Pitino regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Pitino participate in the City of Arcata's consideration of mobilehome
park regulations despite his adult son owning a mobilehome in a mobilehome park?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Pitino from participating in decisions
regarding the property because he has no financial interest in the decisions.

FACTS

The City of Arcata has begun a process of studying, discussing and deliberating on the
adoption of mobilehome park regulations that would include two components:

• A mobilehome rent stabilization ordinance to establish a methodology for City review and
approval of rent increases and

• A mobilehome park conversion, closure and vacancy control ordinance to require advance
City approval prior to any change in use of an existing mobilehome park to another use.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-134
Page No. 2

Councilmember Pitino's son is a resident of one of the mobilehome parks in Arcata that
would be regulated by the proposed ordinances. His son owns a mobilehome and has rented a space
within the park for the last several years. He currently pays approximately $450 in monthly rent.
Mr. Pitino's son is 33 years old and for more than 10 years has been financially independent from
Councilmember Pitino, has not been claimed by Councilmember Pitino as a dependent on his taxes,
and has not received any financial support or assistance from Councilmember Pitino. On May 18,
2017, you stated that the Councilmember's son is also not a source of income to Councilmember
Pitino of any type: direct financial assistance, rental income, etc.

The owner of a different park than the one in which the Councilmember Pitino's son resides
suggested that Councilmember Pitino has a disqualifying conflict of interest because of his son's
tenancy, and that Councilmember Pitino must therefore recuse himself from the City Council's
decision making, discussions and actions pertaining to mobilehome park regulation.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to conflicts of
interests based on the following:

•	Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

•	Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

•	Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

•	Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

•	Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

•	In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-17-134
Page No. 3

Immediate Family

As used in Section 87103, "Immediate family" means the spouse and dependent children.
(Section 82029.) Regulation 18229.1 defines a "dependent child" of a public official as someone
"who is under 18 years old and whom the official is entitled to claim as a dependent on his or her
federal tax return." The Councilmember's 33-year-old son would not be a member of his immediate
family for purposes of Section 87103.

Sources of Income

However, there are other ways that an adult child (or any other person) can be considered a
financial interest. For example, a child may be a source of income to the official pursuant to Section
87103(c) because he provides or promises $500 or more to the official.² You stated that
Councilmember Pitino's son is financially independent from Councilmember Pitino and has not
received any financial support or assistance from Councilmember Pitino, nor has he provided
income to Councilmember Pitino of any type. Based on the facts provided, it does not appear
Councilmember Pitino has any financial interests that would be implicated by the City's
decisionmaking, discussions and actions pertaining to mobilehome park regulations. Thus, he will
not have a conflict of interest in the decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:    John W. Wallace
       Assistant General Counsel
       Legal Division

² Note that under Section 82028(b)(3), gifts from family members, including children (dependent or not), are
not considered gifts for purposes of the Act or Section 87103(e).
